preFix.title: Installing secondary glazing film on draughty single-glazed windows
preFix.subtitle: A low-cost film system that cuts heat loss and draughts from inside
preFix.excerpt: Secondary glazing film, a thin clear film stretched over a window frame with double-sided tape and shrunk tight with a hair dryer, reduces the U-value of a single-glazed window from around 5.7 to roughly 3.4 W/m2K and almost eliminates cold air draughts. The whole house can be done in a day for a small outlay. The film is removed in spring and the process repeated each autumn.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIBSE Guide A for window U-values. Energy Saving Trust window insulation fact sheet 2024. Manufacturer test data for stretched polyolefin film panels.

content.text: Secondary glazing film is the cheapest window insulation available. It traps still air between the film and the glass, reducing the

from around 5.7 (single glazing) to roughly 3.4 W/m2K. That is roughly the same thermal gain as upgrading to basic double glazing, at about one percent of the cost. The film also stops draughts through the glazing bead and poorly fitting sashes, since the air layer is sealed at the perimeter.

Film secondary glazing is seasonal: it must be removed to open the window, and the tape and film are discarded each spring. It is not a substitute for permanent secondary glazing in timber frames or replacement double glazing. In a rented property or a listed building where window replacement is not possible, it is the best available thermal intervention at the window.
Secondary glazing film kit (typically covers 3 to 6 m2 per kit). Kits from any major DIY retailer include film, double-sided tape, and instructions.
Clean cloth and rubbing alcohol (for cleaning the frame before tape application)
Measure the window aperture, the opening from inner frame edge to inner frame edge, not the glass area. Cut the film to the measured size, adding 25 mm extra on all sides.
Clean the inner face of the window frame with rubbing alcohol and allow it to dry fully. The double-sided tape will not adhere to any surface with grease, dust, or moisture.
Apply the double-sided tape to the inner face of the frame, pressing firmly around the full perimeter. Leave the backing paper on for now.
Peel back the backing paper from the tape. Starting at the top, press the film onto the tape. Work down the sides and along the bottom, keeping the film flat and avoiding folds.

Use the hair dryer on a medium heat setting, held 50 to 75 mm from the film, to shrink it tight. Work from the centre outward. The wrinkles disappear as the polyolefin film contracts. Do not hold the dryer still, keep moving to avoid overheating any one spot.
Trim the excess film with scissors close to the tape line. The finished result should be optically clear, like a second pane of glass on the interior.

In spring, peel the film and tape from the frame in one piece. Any remaining adhesive residue comes off with a small amount of rubbing alcohol on a cloth. The frame is unharmed if the tape was the correct type, do not use duct tape or packaging tape, which leave permanent staining.
